---
title: "06 · Systems of Linear Equations"
subtitle: "Two equations, two unknowns — and the geometry that solves them"
lesson-id: "t2.systems-linear-equations"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t2.linear-equations-one-var", "t2.linear-functions-graphs"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)
   and [05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs](../05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)?
2. Can you solve $3x + 5 = 14$?
3. Can you visualise $y = 2x + 1$ as a line on the coordinate plane?
:::

## Touch

Two lines on the coordinate plane. Adjust slope and intercept of each.
Watch their **intersection** (or absence thereof).

```{ojs}
viewof m1 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.5, label: "m₁ (line 1 slope):"})
viewof b1 = Inputs.range([-6, 6], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "b₁ (line 1 intercept):"})
viewof m2 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: -1, step: 0.5, label: "m₂ (line 2 slope):"})
viewof b2 = Inputs.range([-6, 6], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "b₂ (line 2 intercept):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 480, H = 480, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -10, xMax = 10, yMin = -10, yMax = 10;
  const px = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const py = v => H - pad - ((v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * (H - 2 * pad);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:520px; display:block; margin: 0 auto; background:#fafafa;");

  // Grid + axes
  for (let v = xMin; v <= xMax; v++) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(v)).attr("x2", px(v)).attr("y1", pad).attr("y2", H - pad)
      .attr("stroke", v === 0 ? "#1f2933" : "#eee").attr("stroke-width", v === 0 ? 1.5 : 0.5);
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad).attr("y1", py(v)).attr("y2", py(v))
      .attr("stroke", v === 0 ? "#1f2933" : "#eee").attr("stroke-width", v === 0 ? 1.5 : 0.5);
  }

  // Two lines
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(xMin)).attr("y1", py(m1*xMin + b1)).attr("x2", px(xMax)).attr("y2", py(m1*xMax + b1))
    .attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 2.5);
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(xMin)).attr("y1", py(m2*xMin + b2)).attr("x2", px(xMax)).attr("y2", py(m2*xMax + b2))
    .attr("stroke", "#2e7d32").attr("stroke-width", 2.5);

  // Intersection
  let label;
  if (m1 === m2) {
    label = b1 === b2 ? "Same line — infinite solutions" : "Parallel — no solution";
    svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", H - 8).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(label);
  } else {
    const xs = (b2 - b1) / (m1 - m2);
    const ys = m1 * xs + b1;
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(xs)).attr("cy", py(ys)).attr("r", 8).attr("fill", "#1565c0").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", px(xs) + 12).attr("y", py(ys) - 8)
      .attr("font-size", "13").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#1565c0")
      .text(`(${xs.toFixed(1)}, ${ys.toFixed(1)})`);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", H - 8).attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").text(`Unique solution at the intersection`);
  }
  // Equation labels
  const fmt = (m, b, c) => `<tspan fill="${c}" font-family="monospace">y = ${m}x ${b >= 0 ? "+" : "−"} ${Math.abs(b)}</tspan>`;
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", pad + 5).attr("y", 5).attr("width", 220).attr("height", 50).attr("fill", "white").attr("stroke", "#999").attr("rx", 4);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 12).attr("y", 24).attr("font-size", "13").html(fmt(m1, b1, "#c2185b"));
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 12).attr("y", 44).attr("font-size", "13").html(fmt(m2, b2, "#2e7d32"));
  return svg.node();
}
```

A **system** of two linear equations asks: "Is there a point that lies
on **both** lines?" Three possibilities: lines cross at one point
(unique solution), lines are parallel (no solution), lines coincide
(infinite solutions).

## Wonder

1. Set $m_1 = m_2 = 1$ and $b_1 = 2, b_2 = 3$. The lines are *parallel*.
   Could they ever meet? What does this say about the system's solution?
2. Set $m_1 = m_2 = 1$ and $b_1 = b_2 = 2$. Now what?
3. The intersection point — what's true about it in terms of *both*
   equations?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Solve, by **substitution**:
$$y = 2x + 1, \qquad y = -x + 7.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Both right-hand sides equal $y$, so they equal each other.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x + 1 = -x + 7 \Rightarrow 3x = 6 \Rightarrow x = 2$.
Then $y = 2 \cdot 2 + 1 = 5$. **Solution: $(2, 5)$**.

Check both equations: $5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$ ✓; $5 = -2 + 7$ ✓.

The substitution method: when one variable is *isolated* in one
equation, plug into the other. The two-equation system collapses to
one equation in one unknown.
</details>

### Problem 2

Solve, by **elimination**:
$$3x + 2y = 16, \qquad 5x - 2y = 8.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The $y$-coefficients are $+2$ and $-2$ — they cancel if you *add* the
equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Add the two equations: $(3x + 2y) + (5x - 2y) = 16 + 8 \Rightarrow 8x =
24 \Rightarrow x = 3$. Substitute into either equation: $3 \cdot 3 + 2y
= 16 \Rightarrow 2y = 7 \Rightarrow y = 3.5$.

**Solution: $(3, 3.5)$.** Check: $5 \cdot 3 - 2 \cdot 3.5 = 15 - 7 = 8$
✓.

The elimination method: when coefficients of one variable are opposite
(or you can make them so by multiplying), adding eliminates that
variable. Two variables → one variable → solved.
</details>

### Problem 3

Solve:
$$2x + 3y = 12, \qquad 4x + 6y = 25.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply the first equation by 2. What do you get? How does it
compare to the second?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Doubling the first: $4x + 6y = 24$. But the second says $4x + 6y = 25$.
The same expression cannot equal both 24 and 25 — **no solution**.

Geometrically: both equations define lines with slope $-\tfrac{2}{3}$
but different intercepts → **parallel lines**. They never cross.
</details>

### Problem 4

Solve:
$$x + y = 5, \qquad 2x + 2y = 10.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compare the two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The second is exactly *twice* the first. They are the same equation in
disguise — **the same line** — so every point on it satisfies both.
**Infinitely many solutions** along the line $x + y = 5$.
</details>

### Problem 5

A coffee shop sells **lattes for Rs. 80 each** and **cookies for Rs.
30 each**. One morning, the owner sells **20 items in total** and
collects **Rs. 1100**. How many of each were sold?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let $\ell$ = number of lattes, $c$ = number of cookies. Two equations:
total items, total revenue.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\ell + c = 20$ and $80\ell + 30c = 1100$.

From the first: $c = 20 - \ell$. Substitute: $80\ell + 30(20 - \ell) =
1100 \Rightarrow 80\ell + 600 - 30\ell = 1100 \Rightarrow 50\ell = 500
\Rightarrow \ell = 10$. Then $c = 10$.

**10 lattes and 10 cookies.** Check: $10 + 10 = 20$ ✓; $800 + 300 =
1100$ ✓.

Word problems with two unknowns translate naturally into two-equation
systems. The skill is *naming* the unknowns and *writing the two
constraints*.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The systematic *elimination* method you used in Problem 2 is recorded
in **Chapter 8 of the ancient Chinese text *The Nine Chapters on the
Mathematical Art*** (compiled around 100 CE), under the title
*Fang-cheng* — "rectangular arrays." The 3rd-century commentator **Liu
Hui** explained the algorithm clearly. It is essentially what the West
later rediscovered and now calls **Gaussian elimination** — almost two
millennia before Gauss.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A solution to a system is a point that lives on *both* lines at once.
Either there is exactly one such point, or none, or the two lines are
secretly the same line — three cases, no others.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **system of two linear equations in two unknowns** is a pair
$$\begin{aligned} a_1 x + b_1 y &= c_1 \\ a_2 x + b_2 y &= c_2 \end{aligned}$$
A **solution** is a pair $(x, y)$ satisfying *both*.

**Three cases (geometric and algebraic):**

| Geometry | Algebra | Solutions |
|---|---|---|
| Lines cross at one point | Different slopes | Exactly one |
| Lines parallel | Same slope, different intercepts | None |
| Same line | Same slope and intercept (one is multiple of other) | Infinitely many |

**Two solving methods:**

- **Substitution:** isolate one variable in one equation, plug into the
  other.
- **Elimination:** add or subtract scaled versions of the equations to
  cancel one variable.

Choose whichever is easier given the equations' shape.

:::

> *Two lines: cross once, never, or always — the three faces of a system.*
> *Substitute when a variable is alone; eliminate when coefficients align.*
> *The intersection is the solution.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve:
> $$2x + 3y = 13, \qquad x - y = 1.$$

A competent solver: the second equation is simple — solve for $x$ and
substitute.

1. From $x - y = 1$: $x = y + 1$.
2. Substitute into the first: $2(y + 1) + 3y = 13 \Rightarrow 2y + 2 +
   3y = 13 \Rightarrow 5y = 11 \Rightarrow y = 2.2$.
3. Then $x = y + 1 = 3.2$.
4. Check: $2 \cdot 3.2 + 3 \cdot 2.2 = 6.4 + 6.6 = 13$ ✓.

**Solution: $(3.2, 2.2)$.** Real-world systems often produce non-whole
solutions; the algebra is honest about it.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve $y = 3x, y = 2x + 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x = 2x + 4 \Rightarrow x = 4$, $y = 12$. **(4, 12)**.
</details>

**W2.** Solve $x + y = 10, x - y = 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add the two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x = 14 \Rightarrow x = 7, y = 3$. **(7, 3)**.
</details>

**W3.** What kind of solution does $2x + y = 6, 4x + 2y = 12$ have?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The second is twice the first → same line → **infinitely many
solutions**.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $3x + 2y = 16, 2x - y = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
From the second: $y = 2x - 1$. Substitute.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 2(2x - 1) = 16 \Rightarrow 7x - 2 = 16 \Rightarrow x = 18/7$. Then
$y = 2 \cdot 18/7 - 1 = 29/7$. **(18/7, 29/7)**, fractions are fine.
</details>

**P2.** A boatman rows downstream **20 km in 2 hours** and back
**upstream in 5 hours**. Find the speed of the boat in still water and
the speed of the current.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let boat speed = $b$, current speed = $c$. Downstream speed = $b + c$,
upstream = $b - c$. Both distances are 20 km.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$b + c = 20/2 = 10$ km/h. $b - c = 20/5 = 4$ km/h. Add: $2b = 14
\Rightarrow b = 7$. Subtract: $2c = 6 \Rightarrow c = 3$.

**Boat speed: 7 km/h. Current: 3 km/h.** A classic upstream-downstream
problem; the system structure makes it tidy.
</details>

**P3.** Solve $5x + 4y = 22, 3x - 2y = 6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply the second equation by 2 to make $y$-coefficients ±4. Then
add.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 \times$ second: $6x - 4y = 12$. Add to first: $11x = 34 \Rightarrow
x = 34/11$. Then from second: $3 \cdot 34/11 - 2y = 6 \Rightarrow 2y =
102/11 - 6 = 36/11 \Rightarrow y = 18/11$. **(34/11, 18/11)**.

Elimination requires careful scaling but works on any system.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** When we solved a one-input neural network in
[02 P3](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd) by inspection, we
had: $2w + b = 7$ and $4w + b = 11$. Solve this two-equation system
formally using elimination.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Subtract first from second: $2w = 4 \Rightarrow w = 2$. Then $b = 7 -
2 \cdot 2 = 3$. **$w = 2, b = 3$**, agreeing with the earlier
inspection.

The "training" of the simplest neural network is *literally* solving
this system. Real networks have millions of weights and millions of
data points — far too many for direct solution — so they use iterative
methods (gradient descent, Tier 6). But every iteration is moving
toward the solution of a system *just like this one*.
</details>

**S2.** Three lines, three equations, three unknowns: solve
$$x + y + z = 6, \qquad x - y + z = 2, \qquad 2x + y - z = 1.$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add the first and third: eliminates $z$. Subtract second from first:
eliminates $x$ and $z$. Find $y$ from one of these, then continue.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**First minus second:** $(x+y+z) - (x-y+z) = 6 - 2 \Rightarrow 2y = 4
\Rightarrow y = 2$.
**First plus third:** $3x + 2y = 7$. With $y = 2$: $3x + 4 = 7
\Rightarrow x = 1$.
**Substitute back into first:** $1 + 2 + z = 6 \Rightarrow z = 3$.

**Solution: $(1, 2, 3)$.** Check all three equations.

The same elimination idea scales. With $n$ equations in $n$ unknowns,
the systematic version is called **Gaussian elimination** — the
backbone of linear algebra (Tier 5) and of every modern numerical
solver.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Two equations always have one solution."** Not always: parallel
   lines give *no* solution; coincident lines give *infinite*
   solutions.
2. **"Substitution and elimination give different answers."** No —
   they give the same answer (algebraic equivalence). Choose whichever
   is easier given the equations' structure.
3. **"All systems can be solved by hand."** True for 2×2 and 3×3, but
   computational methods take over for large systems. Linear algebra
   (Tier 5) and numerical methods (Tier 6) provide the tools.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [system of linear equations](/glossary.qmd#system-of-linear-equations)
   asks for the point that satisfies *every* equation at once — the
   intersection of the lines.**
2. **Two methods, one answer:
   [substitution](/glossary.qmd#substitution-method) when a variable is
   isolated; [elimination](/glossary.qmd#elimination-method) when
   coefficients can be made to cancel.**
3. **Three outcomes, always: one solution, none, or infinitely many —
   the lines cross, miss, or coincide.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you sketch what "no solution", "one solution", and
   "infinitely many solutions" look like geometrically for two lines?
2. Without scrolling back, can you solve $x + y = 7, 2x - y = 5$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $3x = 12 \Rightarrow x = 4, y = 3$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[07 · Exponents and Powers](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (3-variable system).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)**
- **[05 · Linear Functions and Their Graphs](../05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 5 — Linear Algebra** — generalises to systems of *any* size,
  with matrices and Gaussian elimination as the central tools.
- **Tier 6 — Optimisation** — when constraints are inequalities, you
  get *linear programming*.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Training a linear model is solving a
system: find weights satisfying (or fitting) the data equations.
Every iteration of gradient descent moves toward a solution.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Many cryptographic problems reduce to *solving
systems modulo a large prime* — a number-theoretic version of what
you just did.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Engineering & physics.** Circuit analysis (Kirchhoff's laws),
structural mechanics (force balance), chemical equilibrium — all
become systems of linear equations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Mixture problems (recipe scaling, chemistry),
break-even analysis, scheduling — anywhere two unknowns interact
under two constraints.
:::
